title: 💡 The Real Electricity Cost of a Fully Smart Home
description:
Smart homes are convenient. They also use electricity around the clock. In this video I break down the actual annual cost of running a fully smart home in 2026, with measurements from real devices, and the practical steps to keep your bill manageable while staying connected.

Smart home, electricity cost, energy efficiency, smart device standby, IoT 2026, home automation, energy monitoring.

segments.voice: Smart homes have a hidden cost most users never calculate. The constant power draw of every connected device. Always-on Wi-Fi, processors waiting for commands, sensors monitoring twenty-four seven. According to a twenty twenty-four Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory study, the average smart home with twenty plus devices uses one hundred forty kilowatt hours per year just for IoT standby. That's twenty-one to thirty dollars annually before any device actually does anything.
segments.visual: smart home devices network glow energy consumption

segments.voice: Let me start with the components. Smart speakers like Echo and HomePod draw two to four watts at idle. Multiply by five speakers in a typical smart home, that's ten to twenty watts continuous. Over a year, eighty-eight to one hundred seventy-five kilowatt hours just for speakers waiting to hear wake words. Thirteen to twenty-six dollars per year on speakers alone.
segments.visual: smart speakers idle wattage measurement

segments.voice: Smart bulbs are the next layer. An LED smart bulb in standby mode draws zero point five to one point five watts even when off, just to maintain Wi-Fi or Zigbee connection. Twenty smart bulbs throughout a home, that's ten to thirty watts continuous. Annual standby cost, fourteen to forty dollars. The bulbs are saving energy when on versus traditional, but the standby cost partially offsets the savings.
segments.visual: smart bulb off standby measurement watts

segments.voice: Here's the catch. Cheaper smart bulbs often have higher standby draw. The Wiz and Sengled budget bulbs measured at one point three watts standby in twenty twenty-four iFixit testing, while the Hue and Nanoleaf premium bulbs measured at zero point six. The cheap bulbs save money on purchase, lose money on standby, over the bulb's lifetime the savings often even out.
segments.visual: bulb brand comparison standby consumption

segments.voice: Smart hubs and bridges. The SmartThings Station draws four to six watts continuously. The Aqara M3 hub draws two to three watts. Home Assistant on a Raspberry Pi four draws three to five watts. The Apple TV at idle draws six to nine watts. Most homes have at least one of these running twenty-four seven. Add fifteen to thirty kilowatt hours per year per device.
segments.visual: hub bridge devices wattage cards comparison

segments.voice: Smart cameras are the worst offenders for power. A typical Wi-Fi smart camera draws three to seven watts continuously. With night vision active, six to twelve watts. Five smart cameras at five watts average is twenty-five watts continuous, two hundred nineteen kilowatt hours per year, thirty-three to fifty dollars annually. Cameras dominate smart home power budgets.
segments.visual: security camera Wi-Fi night vision power draw

segments.voice: Smart plugs are the lightest. Most modern smart plugs draw zero point five to one watt. Even with twenty smart plugs in a home, total continuous draw is ten to twenty watts. Fourteen to thirty dollars annually. The least power-hungry smart device category. Buy as many as you need without worrying about adding to your bill significantly.
segments.visual: smart plugs minimal draw efficient

segments.voice: Now the actual home calculation. Twenty smart bulbs at one watt average, twenty watts. Eight smart plugs at zero point seven watts, six watts. Five Wi-Fi smart cameras at five watts, twenty-five watts. Three smart speakers at three watts, nine watts. One smart hub at four watts. Internet router and modem twenty watts. Total continuous draw, eighty-four watts. That's seven hundred thirty-six kilowatt hours per year.
segments.visual: calculation total smart home wattage chart

segments.voice: At an average US electricity rate of fifteen cents per kilowatt hour, that's one hundred ten dollars per year. In high-cost states like California, Hawaii, or New York at twenty-five to thirty cents per kilowatt hour, the same setup costs one hundred eighty-five to two hundred ten dollars per year. The location dependency is significant.
segments.visual: state electricity rates map cost variations

segments.voice: But the ROI looks different when you count the savings. The same smart home that costs one hundred ten dollars per year in standby also delivers savings. Smart thermostat at one hundred sixty dollars annually. Smart plugs eliminating vampire power, eighty dollars. Smart blinds reducing cooling, fifty dollars. Total savings around two hundred ninety dollars. Net positive of one hundred eighty dollars per year, even after standby.
segments.visual: savings versus standby calculation balance

segments.voice: Now let's discuss reduction strategies. Strategy one, audit your devices. Use a Kill A Watt meter at fifteen dollars or a smart plug with energy monitoring to measure each smart device's actual consumption. The numbers in this video are averages. Your specific devices may be twice as efficient or twice as wasteful. Knowing the actual draw enables specific decisions.
segments.visual: Kill A Watt meter measurement device testing

segments.voice: Strategy two, eliminate redundant smart speakers. Many homes have an Echo, a HomePod, and a Google Nest in the same room. Each draws three to four watts continuously. Pick one ecosystem and remove the others. The convenience of having multiple voice assistants rarely justifies the ongoing cost.
segments.visual: multiple voice assistants redundancy single ecosystem

segments.voice: Strategy three, replace Wi-Fi smart devices with mesh protocol versions where possible. A Zigbee or Thread smart bulb draws roughly the same standby power as Wi-Fi, but the network overhead is lower because each device doesn't need to maintain a Wi-Fi handshake. The savings are smaller per device but compound across many devices.
segments.visual: Wi-Fi versus mesh protocol efficiency

segments.voice: Strategy four, schedule downtime for cameras. Indoor cameras don't need to be on twenty-four hours a day in many situations. Set them to active only when nobody is home, or only at night. Several smart camera platforms support time-based or location-based privacy modes. Even cutting active time by fifty percent saves a noticeable percentage of camera power consumption.
segments.visual: camera schedule privacy mode active hours

segments.voice: Strategy five, replace older smart hubs and devices. The fifth generation Echo Dot draws less than half the power of the third generation. The new Hue Bridge draws less than the older one. As smart home technology matures, efficiency improves. A device older than five years is often worth replacing on energy savings alone, especially for always-on hubs.
segments.visual: old versus new generation efficiency comparison

segments.voice: Strategy six, consolidate hubs. Running both a SmartThings Station and a separate Hue Bridge and a separate Aqara hub adds up to ten plus watts of always-on power. Consolidating to a single multi-protocol hub like SmartThings or Home Assistant cuts this in half. The migration takes time but the long-term efficiency gain is real.
segments.visual: hub consolidation single multi protocol setup

segments.voice: Solar offset. If you have solar panels, the smart home standby cost is much less significant because much of the day-time draw is offset by your own production. The night-time portion still pulls from the grid or battery, but the round-trip cost is roughly half what a solar-less home pays. Smart home and solar are mutually reinforcing investments.
segments.visual: solar panels home offsetting daytime power

segments.voice: In my own setup, I measured every always-on device with energy monitoring smart plugs and a Sense whole-home monitor. The total continuous draw came in at sixty-three watts. Five hundred fifty-two kilowatt hours per year. About eighty-three dollars annually at my rate. Lower than the average because I optimized aggressively after measuring.
segments.visual: personal setup measurement results optimized

segments.voice: What surprised me. The Wi-Fi mesh router I use, an Eero six pro three pack, drew thirty-six watts continuously. Three hundred fifteen kilowatt hours per year just for Wi-Fi infrastructure. About forty-seven dollars at my rate. Replacing with a more efficient pair of wired access points cut this to fifteen watts and saved twenty-five dollars annually.
segments.visual: router mesh Wi-Fi power consumption surprise

segments.voice: The trade-off matrix. Reducing smart home power consumption sometimes reduces functionality. Cameras that are scheduled off can miss events. Cheaper ecosystems with less always-on overhead may have weaker automation. A Home Assistant on a Raspberry Pi four uses less power than a Home Assistant Yellow but has more failure modes. Pick your trade-offs deliberately, not by default.
segments.visual: trade off matrix power versus features

segments.voice: Specific high-efficiency picks for twenty twenty-six. The Aqara P3 motion sensor at zero point one watts standby. The Tapo P125M plug at zero point five watts. The Apple HomePod mini at two watts. The Hue Hub at one point five watts. Reolink Argus four cameras with solar panels at two watts effective grid draw. Building around these starts your home with low overhead.
segments.visual: efficient device picks specific brands list

segments.voice: Specific high-consumption picks to avoid or supplement with. Older Ring Stick Up Cams at six to eight watts. Older Samsung TVs that wake networks even at standby, four to seven watts. Older Echo Show series with always-on screens at six to ten watts. Replace these with newer generations or supplement with smart plugs that turn them fully off when not needed.
segments.visual: high consumption devices replace upgrade

segments.voice: Insurance for the long-haul. Track your smart home electricity usage quarterly. Use the Sense or Emporia Vue or just monthly utility comparisons. Trends matter more than absolute numbers. If your standby creep is increasing year over year, you're adding devices faster than you're optimizing existing ones. Awareness alone keeps the cost in check.
segments.visual: tracking quarterly graph trend monitoring

segments.voice: What about smart appliances. A smart refrigerator draws roughly the same power as a non-smart refrigerator. The smart features add maybe one to two watts. A smart oven adds three to five watts in standby for the connectivity module. A smart washing machine adds one to two watts. The added consumption is small relative to the base appliance draw.
segments.visual: smart appliances additional standby small

segments.voice: Final framing. A typical smart home adds about eighty to one hundred kilowatt hours per year over a non-smart home, costing twelve to thirty dollars annually depending on your rate. The savings from smart automation can offset this several times over for most households. The investment is generally net positive but worth measuring rather than assuming.
segments.visual: final framing investment net positive measure

segments.voice: Subscribe to EspacioInteligente and hit the bell. Next week's setup goes deeper into smart irrigation systems and how they save water in drought-prone areas like California, Texas, and Arizona. Drop a comment with your monthly electric bill, and I will help identify the highest-impact smart home optimizations for your situation.
segments.visual: subscribe button notification bell youtube channel
